data.source: Laboratory experiments with undergraduate students at Arizona State University
data.wave_or_subset: Three experimental treatments; 2-person and 5-person group configurations conducted between November 2007 and February 2008
data.notes: Tokens represented monetary units; games framed as downloading files to avoid irrigation bias; asymmetric access positions labelled A–E.

method.description: Participants were allotted tokens each round to invest in a joint ‘bandwidth’ infrastructure that generated a common-pool resource. After the investment stage, participants sequentially downloaded files, with upstream positions having priority access. Experiments were run for ten rounds under three different treatments and in both 2-person and 5-person groups. Behavioural data were analysed with hierarchical linear models and non-parametric tests to examine how position, communication and round affected investments and earnings.
steps: Recruit undergraduate subjects and assign them to 2-person or 5-person groups with labelled positions (A is most upstream).
Provide each participant with 10 tokens per round (worth $0.10 each).
Stage 1 (Investment): each participant simultaneously decides how many tokens to invest in public infrastructure (bandwidth).
Compute total investment and translate it into bandwidth using a sigmoidal production function (inflection at 12 tokens for N=2, 30 tokens for N=5).
Stage 2 (Extraction): participants sequentially download digital files using the generated bandwidth; upstream players move first.
Pay monetary earnings each round equal to uninvested tokens plus tokens earned from downloaded files.
Repeat for ten rounds; enable communication between rounds in specified treatments.
Record investments, downloads and earnings for every individual and round.
Analyse data with hierarchical linear (mixed) models and Wilcoxon matched-pairs signed-ranks tests, controlling for individual and group effects.
method.models: Hierarchical linear models; Wilcoxon Matched-Pairs Signed-Ranks Tests
method.outcome_variable: Tokens invested in public infrastructure; tokens earned per round; number of files downloaded
method.independent_variables: Player position (upstream vs downstream), round number, previous round share of resource, gender, academic major, treatment (communication, file-loss rules)
method.control_variables: Group-level random effects; individual-level random effects

results.summary: Upstream participants appropriated more resources and earned higher profits than downstream participants, confirming positional asymmetry. Despite a Nash prediction of zero investment, individuals invested substantial portions of their endowment, and communication significantly boosted cooperation. Tail-end participants punished upstream over-extraction by lowering later investments, reducing overall infrastructure provision. Coordination became more difficult as group size increased, and inequality in earnings dampened investment in larger (5-person) groups.
